content:
# Acts Chapter 27 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 27 (The Shipwreck)

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "it was decided" construction | "And when it was decided [for] us to sail-away into Italy" | "And when it was decided that we should sail to Italy" | "When it was decided that we would sail for Italy" | Adopted LOGOS: "And when it was decided that we should sail to Italy" -- retains the conjunction "And" (kai in Greek) that PNEUMA drops, and uses natural English without EMET's bracketed supplements. |
| 1 | Verb for transfer of prisoners | "they were handing-over both Paul" | "they delivered Paul" | "Paul and some other prisoners were handed over" | Adopted LOGOS "delivered" as a clean aorist rendering; EMET's imperfect "were handing-over" over-reads the tense; PNEUMA restructures to passive which obscures the Greek subject. |
| 1 | Military unit designation | "Augustan cohort" | "Augustan Cohort" | "Imperial Regiment" | Adopted LOGOS "Augustan Cohort" -- Sebastes is the Greek equivalent of Augustus and should be transliterated; PNEUMA's "Imperial Regiment" is interpretive. |
| 2 | "embarked on / boarded" | "having embarked on a ship of Adramyttium" | "embarking in a ship of Adramyttium" | "We boarded a ship from Adramyttium" | Adopted LOGOS phrasing with participial construction preserving Greek syntax; PNEUMA's finite verb restructures. |
| 2 | Destination description | "being-about to sail to the places along Asia" | "which was about to sail to the ports along the coast of Asia" | "about to make stops along the coast of the province of Asia" | Adopted LOGOS: "ports along the coast of Asia" -- EMET's "places along" is too vague; PNEUMA's "province of Asia" adds an interpretive gloss. |
| 3 | Verb for arrival at Sidon | "we were brought-down to Sidon" | "we put in at Sidon" | "we landed at Sidon" | Adopted LOGOS "put in at" -- a proper nautical term; EMET's "brought-down" is overly literal (katechthemen); PNEUMA's "landed" is acceptable but less precise. |
| 3 | Julius's treatment of Paul | "having treated Paul with-human-kindness" (philanthropos) | "treating Paul kindly" | "treated Paul with kindness" | Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus of "treating Paul with kindness"; EMET's "with-human-kindness" preserves the Greek philanthropos but is awkward in English. |
| 3 | What Paul received | "permitted [him] to go to [his] friends [and] to obtain care" | "permitted him to go to his friends and receive their care" | "allowing him to visit his friends and receive their care" | Adopted LOGOS: "permitted him to go to his friends and receive their care" -- EMET's "obtain care" is unusual; PNEUMA's "visit" softens the Greek poreuomai. |
| 4 | "put to sea" phrasing | "having put-to-sea" | "putting to sea from there" | "put out to sea again" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "again" not in Greek. |
| 5 | Verb for sea crossing | "having sailed-through the sea along Cilicia" | "having sailed across the open sea along Cilicia" | "After crossing the open sea off the coast of Cilicia" | Adopted LOGOS: "having sailed across the open sea" -- preserves participle; PNEUMA restructures with "After crossing." EMET's "sailed-through" is wooden. |
| 5 | Arrival verb | "we came-down to Myra of Lycia" | "we came to Myra in Lycia" | "we landed at Myra in Lycia" | Adopted LOGOS "we came to Myra in Lycia"; EMET preserves katēlthomen as "came-down" (over-literal); PNEUMA's "landed" is interpretive. |
| 6 | Ship description | "a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy" | "a ship of Alexandria sailing to Italy" | "an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "Alexandrian ship bound for" restructures the genitive. |
| 6 | "put us into it / aboard it" | "put us into it" | "put us aboard it" | "put us aboard it" | Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA majority: "put us aboard it." |
| 7 | Time description | "in a considerable [number] of days" | "for a good many days" | "for many days" | Adopted LOGOS: "for a good many days" -- captures hikanais naturally; EMET is overly literal; PNEUMA drops the intensifier. |
| 7 | Difficulty arriving | "with-difficulty having come off Cnidus" | "having arrived with difficulty off Cnidus" | "had difficulty arriving off Cnidus" | Adopted LOGOS as most balanced rendering. |
| 7 | Wind preventing further progress | "the wind not permitting us [further]" | "the wind not permitting us to go farther" | "When the wind would not let us hold our course" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA paraphrases with "hold our course" (not in Greek). |
| 8 | "coasting along" difficulty | "with-difficulty coasting-along it" | "coasting along it with difficulty" | "Struggling along the coast" | Adopted LOGOS word order; PNEUMA's "Struggling" is interpretive. |
| 8 | "city" vs "town" | "city of Lasea" | "city of Lasea" | "town of Lasea" | Adopted EMET/LOGOS: "city" -- Greek polis means city. |
| 9 | Time description | "considerable time having passed" | "when considerable time had passed" | "By now much time had been lost" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "lost" (interpretive); EMET uses genitive absolute directly. |
| 9 | "admonishing" vs "advised" vs "warned" | "Paul was admonishing [them]" | "Paul advised them" | "Paul warned them" | Adopted PNEUMA's "warned" -- parēnei carries both advisory and cautionary force; "warned" best fits the context of danger ahead. |
| 10 | Speech structure | "saying to them: Men, I perceive that with injury and much loss..." | "saying, 'Men, I perceive that the voyage will be with injury and much loss...'" | "'Men, I can see that this voyage is heading for disaster and heavy loss...'" | Adopted LOGOS structure; PNEUMA's "heading for disaster" over-interprets; EMET rearranges word order. |
| 10 | "our souls" vs "our lives" | "of our souls" | "of our lives" | "of our lives" | Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA: "our lives" -- psyche in this context means physical life, not soul in the theological sense. |
| 11 | "was being-persuaded" vs "was persuaded" vs "was more persuaded" | "the centurion was being-persuaded" | "the centurion was persuaded" | "the centurion was more persuaded" | Adopted PNEUMA's "was more persuaded" -- the Greek mallon (rather) is comparative and should be reflected; LOGOS omits the comparative nuance; EMET over-renders the imperfect. |
| 11 | "ship-owner" vs "shipowner" vs "ship's owner" | "ship-owner" | "shipowner" | "ship's owner" | Adopted LOGOS: "shipowner" -- single compound word is standard English nautical usage. |
| 12 | Decision verb | "the majority gave counsel to put-to-sea" | "the majority reached a decision to put to sea" | "the majority decided to put out to sea" | Adopted PNEUMA/LOGOS blend: "the majority decided to put to sea" -- natural English. |
| 12 | Phoenix harbor description | "looking toward [the] southwest and toward [the] northwest" | "facing southwest and northwest" | "facing both southwest and northwest" | Adopted LOGOS: "facing southwest and northwest" -- PNEUMA adds "both" (not in Greek). |
| 13 | Wind description | "[the] south-wind having blown gently" | "when a south wind blew gently" | "When a gentle south wind began to blow" | Adopted PNEUMA's "when a gentle south wind began to blow" -- more natural English while preserving meaning; the genitive absolute is awkward in EMET. |
| 13 | "obtained the purpose" | "having supposed [they had] obtained the purpose" | "supposing that they had obtained their purpose" | "they thought they had gotten what they wanted" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "gotten what they wanted" is too colloquial. |
| 13 | "weighed anchor" phrasing | "having weighed [anchor], they were sailing-along Crete close-by" | "they weighed anchor and sailed along Crete, close to the shore" | "they weighed anchor and sailed close along the coast of Crete" | Adopted LOGOS phrasing; "close to the shore" is clearer than PNEUMA's word order. |
| 14 | Storm verb | "struck down from it" | "there rushed down from it" | "swept down from the island" | Adopted consensus "swept down from it" -- PNEUMA's "swept" is vivid and accurate for ebalen; changed "the island" to "it" per Greek pronoun. |
| 15 | Ship seized/caught | "the ship having been seized and not being-able to face the wind, having given [way], we were being carried-along" | "when the ship was caught and could not face the wind, we gave way and were driven along" | "The ship was caught in it and could not face into the wind, so we gave way to it and were driven along" | Adopted LOGOS as most balanced; PNEUMA adds "in it" and "to it" (interpretive supplements); EMET's compound participles are awkward. |
| 16 | "small-island" / boat terminology | "a certain small-island called Cauda, we were-able with-difficulty to become masters of the skiff" | "a small island called Cauda, we were scarcely able to gain control of the ship's boat" | "a small island called Cauda, we were barely able to secure the lifeboat" | Adopted LOGOS: "scarcely able to gain control of the ship's boat" -- EMET's "become masters of the skiff" is overly literal; PNEUMA's "lifeboat" is anachronistic. |
| 17 | Undergirding description | "they were using helps, undergirding the ship" | "they used supports to undergird the ship" | "the crew passed ropes under the ship itself to hold it together" | Adopted LOGOS: "they used supports to undergird the ship" -- technical but clear; PNEUMA's expansion is interpretive; EMET's "helps" is archaic. |
| 17 | Syrtis description | "fearing lest into the Syrtis they should fall" | "fearing that they would run aground on the Syrtis" | "Fearing they would run aground on the sandbars of Syrtis" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "sandbars of" (interpretive gloss). |
| 17 | "lowered the gear" | "having lowered the gear, thus they were being carried-along" | "they lowered the gear and so were driven along" | "they lowered the sea anchor and let the ship be driven along" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA specifies "sea anchor" which is one interpretation of skeuos but not certain. |
| 18 | Storm description | "we being violently storm-tossed, on the next [day] they were making a jettison" | "as we were being violently storm-tossed, on the next day they began to jettison the cargo" | "We were battered so violently by the storm that the next day they began throwing cargo overboard" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA restructures causally ("so...that"); EMET's "making a jettison" is awkward. |
| 19 | "threw out/overboard" | "with-their-own-hands they threw-out the tackle of the ship" | "with their own hands they threw the tackle of the ship overboard" | "they threw the ship's tackle overboard with their own hands" | Adopted LOGOS word order; all convey the same meaning but LOGOS preserves the Greek emphasis (own hands first). |
| 20 | Storm pressing | "no small storm lying-upon [us]" | "no small storm was pressing upon us" | "the storm kept raging without letup" | Adopted LOGOS: "no small storm was pressing upon us" -- preserves the litotes (no small); PNEUMA abandons the Greek construction entirely. |
| 20 | Hope taken away | "all hope of our being-saved was henceforth being taken-away" | "from then on all hope that we would be saved was taken away" | "we finally gave up all hope of being saved" | Adopted LOGOS; the Greek passive periēreito is correctly rendered as hope "was taken away" (not active "gave up" as PNEUMA has it). |
| 21 | "lack-of-appetite" / "without food" | "much lack-of-appetite existing" | "when they had gone a long time without food" | "After the men had gone a long time without eating" | Adopted LOGOS; EMET's "lack-of-appetite" is over-literal for asitia; PNEUMA's "without eating" is slightly less precise. |
| 21 | Paul's speech opening | "O men, it was necessary indeed, having obeyed me, not to have put-to-sea from Crete and to have gained this injury and [the] loss" | "Men, you ought to have listened to me and not have set sail from Crete, and so avoided this injury and loss" | "Men, you should have listened to me and not sailed from Crete. You would have avoided this damage and loss" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA splits into two sentences and uses "damage" for hybrin (better: "injury"); EMET's syntax is convoluted. |
| 22 | "be-of-good-cheer" vs "take heart" vs "take courage" | "I urge you to be-of-good-cheer" | "I urge you to take heart" | "I urge you: take courage!" | Adopted LOGOS: "take heart" -- natural English idiom; EMET's hyphenated compound is awkward; PNEUMA's "take courage" with exclamation is more dynamic than the Greek warrants. |
| 22 | "no casting-away of soul" vs "no loss of life" | "no casting-away of soul from among you" | "no loss of life among you" | "Not one of you will lose your life" | Adopted LOGOS: "no loss of life among you" -- closest to Greek apobole psyches; PNEUMA restructures; EMET's "casting-away of soul" is archaic. |
| 23 | "this night" vs "last night" | "there stood-by me this night" | "there stood by me this night" | "last night an angel...stood beside me" | Adopted EMET/LOGOS: "this night" -- taute te nykti is "this night"; PNEUMA's "last night" is interpretive. |
| 23 | "whose I am" vs "to whom I belong" vs "I belong to" | "the God whose I am and whom I serve" | "the God to whom I belong and whom I serve" | "the God I belong to and serve" | Adopted LOGOS: "the God to whom I belong and whom I serve" -- preserves both relative clauses; PNEUMA drops "whom" before "serve." |
| 24 | "Do not fear" vs "Do not be afraid" | "Do not fear, Paul" | "Do not be afraid, Paul" | "Do not be afraid, Paul" | Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA majority: "Do not be afraid, Paul." |
| 24 | "behold" inclusion | "and behold, God has granted to you all those sailing with you" | "And behold, God has granted you all those who sail with you" | "and God has graciously given you the lives of all who are sailing with you" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA omits "behold" (idou) and adds "graciously" and "the lives of" (not in Greek). |
| 25 | "be-of-good-cheer" / "take heart" / "take courage" | "be-of-good-cheer, men" | "take heart, men" | "take courage, men" | Adopted LOGOS: "take heart, men" -- consistent with v.22 rendering. |
| 25 | "it will be" phrasing | "thus it will be according to the manner it has been spoken to me" | "it will be exactly as it has been told to me" | "it will turn out exactly as I was told" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "turn out" is slightly colloquial; EMET is overly literal. |
| 26 | "upon a certain island we must fall" | "upon a certain island we must fall" | "we must run aground on a certain island" | "we must run aground on some island" | Adopted LOGOS: "we must run aground on a certain island" -- "run aground" correctly interprets ekpesein in nautical context; PNEUMA's "some" is vaguer than Greek tina. |
| 27 | "fourteenth night came" | "when the fourteenth night came" | "when the fourteenth night had come" | "On the fourteenth night" | Adopted LOGOS pluperfect construction; PNEUMA simplifies too much. |
| 27 | "the Adriatic" vs "the Adriatic Sea" | "in the Adriatic" | "in the Adriatic Sea" | "across the Adriatic Sea" | Adopted LOGOS: "in the Adriatic Sea" -- PNEUMA's "across" implies traversal not in the Greek; EMET omits "Sea." |
| 27 | "toward the middle of the night" vs "about midnight" | "toward [the] middle of the night" | "about midnight" | "Around midnight" | Adopted LOGOS: "about midnight" -- natural English; all three render kata meson tes nyktos equivalently. |
| 27 | "suspecting" vs "suspected" vs "sensed" | "the sailors were suspecting some land to be approaching them" | "the sailors suspected that some land was approaching them" | "the sailors sensed that they were approaching land" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA reverses the Greek image (in Greek, the land approaches them); EMET's imperfect "were suspecting" is awkward. |
| 28 | "having sounded" vs "taking soundings" vs "took soundings" | "having sounded, they found twenty fathoms; and having gone-forward a little and again having sounded" | "taking soundings, they found twenty fathoms; and having gone a little farther, they took soundings again" | "They took soundings and found twenty fathoms. A little farther on they took soundings again" | Adopted LOGOS; preserves participial variety while remaining readable. |
| 29 | "rough places" vs "rocky places" vs "rocks" | "against rough places we should fall" | "we might run aground on rocky places" | "we would be dashed against the rocks" | Adopted LOGOS: "rocky places" -- tracheis topous means rough/rocky places; PNEUMA's "dashed against" is dramatic but interpretive. |
| 29 | "cast" vs "dropped" | "having cast four anchors from [the] stern, they were praying [for] day to come" | "they cast four anchors from the stern and prayed for day to come" | "they dropped four anchors from the stern and prayed for daylight" | Adopted LOGOS: "cast" is the proper nautical term; PNEUMA's "daylight" for hemeran is a slight paraphrase. |
| 30 | "skiff" vs "boat" vs "lifeboat" | "having lowered the skiff into the sea" | "had lowered the boat into the sea" | "had lowered the lifeboat into the sea" | Adopted LOGOS: "boat" -- neutral and accurate; "lifeboat" (PNEUMA) is anachronistic; "skiff" (EMET) is archaic. |
| 30 | "extend anchors" vs "lay out anchors" | "from [the] prow being-about to extend anchors" | "they were going to lay out anchors from the bow" | "they were going to lay out anchors from the bow" | Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA: "lay out anchors from the bow" -- standard nautical English. |
| 31 | "these" vs "these men" | "Unless these remain in the ship" | "Unless these men remain in the ship" | "Unless these men stay with the ship" | Adopted LOGOS: "Unless these men remain in the ship" -- PNEUMA's "stay with" softens the Greek menō en. |
| 31 | "you are not able to be saved" vs "you yourselves cannot be saved" | "you are not able to be saved" | "you yourselves cannot be saved" | "you cannot be saved" | Adopted LOGOS: "you yourselves cannot be saved" -- hymeis (emphatic pronoun) is preserved; PNEUMA drops the emphasis. |
| 32 | "cut-away the ropes" vs "cut the ropes" | "the soldiers cut-away the ropes of the skiff" | "the soldiers cut the ropes of the boat" | "the soldiers cut the ropes holding the lifeboat" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "holding" is an addition; EMET's "skiff" is archaic. |
| 33 | "until day was about to come" vs "until day was about to dawn" vs "Just before dawn" | "until [the] day was about to come" | "until the day was about to dawn" | "Just before dawn" | Adopted LOGOS: "until the day was about to dawn" -- achri hou hemera emellen ginesthai; PNEUMA simplifies too much. |
| 33 | "encouraging" vs "urging" | "Paul was encouraging all to partake of food" | "Paul kept urging them all to take food" | "Paul urged them all to eat" | Adopted LOGOS: "kept urging" preserves the imperfect aspect; PNEUMA's aorist rendering loses the continuous urging. |
| 33 | Speech content | "Today [is the] fourteenth day [that] watching you continue without-food, having taken nothing" | "Today is the fourteenth day that you have continued watching and going without food, having taken nothing" | "For fourteen days now you have been in constant suspense and have gone without food -- you have eaten nothing" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "constant suspense" interprets prosdokao beyond its range here; EMET is fragmentary. |
| 34 | "deliverance" vs "preservation" vs "survive" | "this is for your deliverance" | "this is for your preservation" | "You need it to survive" | Adopted LOGOS: "preservation" -- soteria in this context is physical preservation; PNEUMA's "survive" is too colloquial. |
| 34 | "not a hair...will perish" | "not a hair from the head of any of you will perish" | "not a hair from the head of any of you shall perish" | "Not a single hair from any of your heads will be lost" | Adopted LOGOS: "shall perish" preserves the subjunctive force; PNEUMA's "be lost" softens apollymi. |
| 35 | "gave thanks to God before all" vs "in the presence of all" vs "in front of them all" | "he gave-thanks to God before all" | "he gave thanks to God in the presence of all" | "gave thanks to God in front of them all" | Adopted LOGOS: "in the presence of all" -- more dignified; "in front of" (PNEUMA) is colloquial. |
| 36 | "of-good-cheer" vs "encouraged" vs "spirits lifted" | "all having become of-good-cheer, they also themselves took food" | "they all became encouraged and themselves also took food" | "Their spirits lifted, and they all ate as well" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "spirits lifted" is idiomatic but departs from euthumoi genomenoi; EMET is hyphenated. |
| 37 | Phrasing of count | "we were, all the souls in the ship, two hundred seventy-six" | "we were in all two hundred and seventy-six souls in the ship" | "There were 276 of us on board the ship" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA uses numerals and "on board" (informal); EMET's word order is awkward. |
| 38 | "satisfied with food" vs "eaten enough" vs "eaten their fill" | "having been satisfied with food" | "when they had eaten enough" | "When they had eaten their fill" | Adopted LOGOS: "when they had eaten enough" -- koresthentes means satiated; PNEUMA's "their fill" is slightly colloquial. |
| 38 | Lightening verb | "they were lightening the ship, throwing-out the wheat" | "they began to lighten the ship by throwing the wheat" | "they lightened the ship by throwing the grain" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA uses "grain" for siton (wheat is more precise). |
| 39 | Bay/beach description | "a certain bay they were observing having a beach" | "they observed a certain bay having a beach" | "they spotted a bay with a sandy beach" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "sandy" (not in Greek) and "spotted" is colloquial. |
| 39 | "drive out the ship" vs "drive the ship ashore" vs "run the ship aground" | "to drive-out the ship" | "to drive the ship ashore" | "to run the ship aground if they could" | Adopted LOGOS: "to drive the ship ashore" -- exosai means to push out/drive onto; PNEUMA's "run aground" conflates with v.41. |
| 40 | Anchor action | "having cast-off the anchors, they left [them] in the sea" | "casting off the anchors, they left them in the sea" | "They cut the anchors loose and left them in the sea" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "cut...loose" adds a detail not in perielontes (which means "removed/cast off"). |
| 40 | Rudder bands | "having loosened the bands of the rudders" | "loosening the bands of the rudders" | "untied the ropes holding the rudders" | Adopted LOGOS: "loosening the bands of the rudders" -- preserves the technical Greek; PNEUMA interprets "bands" as "ropes" and adds "holding." |
| 40 | "holding [course] toward the beach" vs "made for the beach" vs "headed for the beach" | "they were holding [course] toward the beach" | "they made for the beach" | "headed for the beach" | Adopted LOGOS: "they made for the beach" -- idiomatic and accurate for kateichon. |
| 41 | "place between-two-seas" vs "place where two seas met" vs "sandbar" | "a place between-two-seas" | "a place where two seas met" | "a sandbar" | Adopted LOGOS: "a place where two seas met" -- preserves the Greek image; PNEUMA's "sandbar" is interpretive. |
| 41 | "prow" vs "bow" | "the prow having stuck-fast remained unmoved" | "the prow stuck fast and remained immovable" | "The bow jammed fast and would not move" | Adopted LOGOS with "bow": "the bow stuck fast and remained immovable" -- standard modern nautical English; PNEUMA's "jammed" and "would not move" are colloquial. |
| 41 | "broken-up" vs "broken apart" vs "smashed apart" | "the stern was being broken-up by the force of the waves" | "the stern was being broken apart by the force of the waves" | "the stern was being smashed apart by the pounding waves" | Adopted LOGOS: "broken apart by the force of the waves" -- PNEUMA's "smashed" and "pounding" intensify beyond the Greek. |
| 42 | "plan" vs "plan" / structure | "the plan of the soldiers was that they should kill the prisoners, lest anyone having swum-out should escape" | "the plan of the soldiers was to kill the prisoners, lest any of them should swim away and escape" | "The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners to prevent any of them from swimming away and escaping" | Adopted LOGOS; preserves the noun boule (plan) and the purpose clause with "lest." |
| 43 | "prevented them from the plan" vs "kept them from their purpose/plan" | "prevented them from [the] plan" | "kept them from their purpose" | "kept them from carrying out their plan" | Adopted LOGOS "kept them from their plan" -- blending precision: boulema is "plan/purpose"; PNEUMA adds "carrying out." |
| 43 | "thrown-themselves-off" vs "throw themselves overboard" vs "jump overboard" | "having thrown-themselves-off first, to get upon the land" | "to throw themselves overboard first and get to land" | "to jump overboard first and make for land" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA's "jump" and "make for" are slightly informal. |
| 44 | "some upon some [things] from the ship" vs "pieces from the ship" vs "pieces of the wreckage" | "some upon some [things] from the ship" | "some on pieces from the ship" | "others clinging to pieces of the wreckage" | Adopted LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "clinging" and "wreckage" (not in Greek). |
| 44 | "brought-safely through upon the land" vs "brought safely to land" vs "reached land safely" | "all were brought-safely through upon the land" | "all were brought safely to land" | "everyone reached land safely" | Adopted LOGOS: "all were brought safely to land" -- preserves the divine passive (diasothēnai); PNEUMA's active "reached" loses the theological nuance. |

-- SOPHIA
